1: A direct revelation: faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen. The definition locates faith in the economy of revelation: what faith grasps is not imagination but disclosed reality — the things hoped for are real because God has revealed them; the things not seen are the eschatological realities that the Spirit has given knowledge of. The definition situates the community in the same position as the OT cloud of witnesses: seeing the promised-but-not-yet-received, and holding firm to it as the most real thing in their world.
4: A shadow: Abel's faith-offering that was acceptable to God is the first shadow of the one true acceptable offering. Abel brought the firstborn of his flock — the best, the costly — in faith that God would receive it. Christ is the firstborn of all creation (Colossians 1:15) who offers himself to God as the definitively acceptable sacrifice. Abel's blood cried for justice; Christ's blood speaks the better word of forgiveness (Hebrews 12:24). The shadow-structure: the acceptable-in-faith offering points to the once-for-all offering that is accepted not merely for the offerer but for all who share his faith.
7: A shadow: Noah, warned by God about things not yet seen, built the ark in reverent faith and thereby condemned the world and became heir of righteousness that comes by faith. The pattern: divine warning about judgment not yet visible, faith-obedience that prepares a means of salvation, and the salvation of those who enter. The antitype: Christ warned by the Spirit of the coming judgment (and knowing it himself as the Son), provided by his death the salvation-vessel into which faith enters. Those who believe and are baptized are in the ark; those who do not are condemned by the flood of divine judgment.
17: A type: by faith Abraham, when he was tested, offered up Isaac, and he who had received the promises was in the act of offering up his only son. He considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead, from which, figuratively speaking, he did receive him back. The Aqedah is the supremely explicit OT type of the Father's offering of the Son: Abraham's willingness to give the beloved only son, the divine provision of a substitute (the ram in the thicket), and the receiving-back in a figure all correspond to the Father giving the Son, the Son's substitutionary death, and his resurrection. The type preserves the structure of the atoning act at the deepest level.
26: A shadow: Moses considered the reproach of Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt, for he was looking to the reward. Moses participates in the messianic reproach across the centuries — not because he knew Christ as the community does, but because the reproach of God's people (which Moses took on by identifying with them) is structurally the same as the reproach of the Messiah. The community that bears the reproach of Christ (Hebrews 13:13) enters the same pattern: what Moses began in shadow, the community enacts in the light of the disclosed Messiah.
39: A direct revelation: all these, though commended through their faith, did not receive what was promised, since God had provided something better for us, that apart from us they should not be made perfect. The eschatological communality of salvation: the OT faith-heroes cannot receive the completion of their faith apart from the new covenant community. This is the revelation of the economy of grace: salvation is not individualistic-chronological (each person receives at death) but communal-eschatological (the whole people of God, across all ages, is perfected together at the parousia). The OT witnesses are waiting for the community of Christ to be complete before the common perfection is achieved.
2: A revelation of God: by faith the people of old received their commendation. The OT saints were not commended by works-righteousness or covenant-membership alone but by faith — the same principle that operates in the new covenant. This reveals the unity of the way of salvation across both testaments: Abraham, Abel, Noah, and all the elders were right with God by trusting his word, just as the community of Christ is right with God by trusting the word of the Son. The faith-principle is not new covenant innovation but the constant of covenant relationship.
3: A revelation of God: by faith we understand that the universe was formed by God's word, so that what is seen was not made from visible things. Creation-faith is the foundation of all other faith: God who creates from nothing can fulfill promises that seem impossible. The invisible word produces the visible world — the paradigm of faith's operation. In Christ, the same pattern applies: from the nothing of the sealed tomb, God's word produces the resurrection. Faith in the creator God and faith in the resurrection God are the same trust in the same power.
5: A shadow: Enoch was taken from this life so that he did not experience death — the pattern: faith, pleasing God, escaping death. This is the OT shadow of what Christ achieves definitively: by his obedient death he destroys the power of death for all who are in him (Hebrews 2:14). Enoch bypassed death as an individual exception; Christ conquers death as the head of a new humanity. The translation of Enoch is the shadow; the resurrection of Christ and the eschatological transformation of the community at his return (1 Corinthians 15:52) is the fulfillment.
6: A revelation of God: without faith it is impossible to please God, because anyone who comes to him must believe that he exists and that he rewards those who seek him. The structure of relationship to God is disclosed as inherently relational (to a God who is, who sees, who responds) and inherently eschatological (seeking him as the rewarder). This is not works-based seeking but faith-seeking: trusting that the unseen God is real and that his promised reward will come. Christ is the supreme expression of the rewarder: in him, the one who believes receives eternal life (John 3:16).
8: A shadow: by faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to go to a place he would later receive as his inheritance, not knowing where he was going. Abraham's faith-obedience to a call without full disclosure of the destination is the shadow of Christian discipleship: called, baptized, following, not yet seeing the city but trusting the one who called. The father of faith models the pattern of new covenant life — stepping forward on the word of God without the ability to verify the destination by sight. The community of Christ follows the same path.
9: A theme: by faith Abraham lived in the promised land as a stranger in a foreign country, living in tents, as did Isaac and Jacob. The theme of sojourning — possessing the promise while living as a foreigner — runs from Abraham through the OT community to the new covenant community (1 Peter 2:11). The promised land was never the final home; the heavenly country was (Hebrews 11:16). Christ is the one in whom the final homeland is secured: he has gone ahead (John 14:3) and prepared a place, and the community lives in the present world as strangers looking toward it.
10: A shadow: Abraham was looking for the city with foundations, whose architect and builder is God. Abraham's forward-looking faith toward the eternal city is the OT shadow of the eschatological reality Christ inaugurates. The city with foundations (Revelation 21:10-14) is the new Jerusalem that descends from heaven — built not by human hands but by the God who builds through Christ's redemption. Abraham saw its outline from afar; the community sees the architect's face in Christ and holds the promise of the city more clearly than Abraham could from his tent.
11: A revelation of God: by faith even Sarah received ability to conceive because she considered him faithful who had promised. The character of the promising God is disclosed: he gives life to the barren, ability to the unable, fulfillment to the apparently-broken promise. The same God raises the dead (Hebrews 11:19), fulfills covenants against biological impossibility, and raises Christ. The pattern of impossible-fulfilled-by-divine-faithfulness is the shape of all redemptive history, culminating in the resurrection of the crucified Son.
12: A revelation of God: from one man, as good as dead, came descendants as numerous as the stars of the sky and the sand on the seashore. The multiplication from the as-good-as-dead (nenekrōmenou) Abraham reveals the resurrection-pattern: from death and deadness God alone produces an abundance of life. In Christ, from the one who died and was raised, comes the innumerable community of faith from all nations (Revelation 7:9). The logic of Abraham's biological resurrection is the logic of the community's eschatological resurrection in the Son.
13: A direct revelation: all these died in faith not having received the things promised; they only saw and greeted them from afar. The OT faithful died without receiving the fullness of the promise — it remained eschatologically ahead of them. This is Hebrews' key to reading both testaments: the OT community held the promise in faith-sight; the NT community holds the fulfillment in Christ, but still awaits the final realization (Hebrews 11:40). Both communities are faith-communities in the sense of holding what is real but not yet fully visible — the former looking forward, the latter looking both back (to the cross) and forward (to the parousia).
14: A theme: people who say such things show they are looking for a country of their own. The OT pilgrims' self-description as strangers and foreigners is their confession that this world is not their final country. The theme of the sought-homeland runs through the entire biblical narrative: from Eden to the city with foundations, God's people are always moving toward a country they cannot yet see. The community of Christ lives in the same orientation — the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:2) is their homeland, and Christ who has already entered it (Hebrews 9:24) is their forerunner.
15: A revelation of God: if they had been thinking of the country they left, they would have had opportunity to return. The OT saints could have returned to Ur, to Egypt, to earthly security — but they chose the forward-looking faith instead. Their choice discloses the structure of faith: it is not nostalgia for the pre-faith life but trust in the promised future. The community of Christ similarly has opportunities to "return" to pre-conversion identities and securities, but the faith that saw the heavenly country makes the return impossible without abandoning what was seen.
16: A direct revelation: they were longing for a better country — a heavenly one. Therefore God is not ashamed to be called their God, for he has prepared a city for them. The OT faithful's longing for the heavenly country is the explicit disclosure that the entire OT narrative was always oriented beyond the earthly promised land. God is not ashamed to be called their God because he has actually prepared the city — the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:10) — through Christ's redemption. The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob is the God who raised Jesus from the dead (Matthew 22:31-32), validating this heavenly hope.
18: A type: "It is through Isaac that your offspring will be reckoned" — the divine word that made Isaac the vessel of the covenant promise is the type of the specificity of the covenant in Christ. The promise comes through the one designated by God, not through human logic or natural descent. As the promise came through the "impossible" Isaac born to the barren and aged, it comes through the crucified and risen Christ — the one whom death should have ended but whom God raised (Galatians 3:16). The type preserves the pattern of divine specificity against natural impossibility.
19: A type: Abraham reasoned that God could raise the dead, and so in a manner of speaking (en parabolē) he did receive Isaac back. The Aqedah is the OT parable of resurrection: Abraham's trust that God could raise is the shadow of what actually happened in Christ's literal resurrection. Isaac "received back" from virtual death is the figure whose literal fulfillment is the empty tomb. The faith that trusts a God who raises the dead finds its ultimate validation in the resurrection of the Son, and those who share Abraham's resurrection-reasoning share his justification (Romans 4:24-25).
20: A theme: by faith Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau in regard to their future. Isaac's faith-blessing looks beyond what the eye sees to what God's promise will make. The theme of the covenant-blessing transmitted through the chosen line anticipates the Christological reality: the blessing of Abraham (Galatians 3:14) comes through the seed who is Christ. Isaac's faith-act participates in the forward movement of the covenant promise through unexpected channels — Jacob over Esau — the pattern of divine election running contrary to natural expectation that culminates in the crucified Messiah.
21: A theme: by faith Jacob, when he was dying, blessed each of Joseph's sons and worshipped as he leaned on his staff. Jacob's faith-blessing at death — crossing his hands to place the greater blessing on the younger (Genesis 48:14) — continues the pattern of God's surprising election running contrary to human convention. The blessing flows through the unexpected one, as it does ultimately in Christ: the stone the builders rejected (Psalms 118:22) becomes the cornerstone; the firstborn from the dead who was crucified in apparent weakness inherits all things (Hebrews 1:2; Colossians 1:18).
22: A shadow: by faith Joseph, when he was dying, spoke of the exodus of the Israelites and gave instructions about his bones. Joseph's dying faith-instruction points two directions: his bones were to participate in the great deliverance (Exodus 13:19), and his faith anticipated the exodus as the paradigmatic act of divine rescue. The exodus itself is the shadow of the greater exodus Christ accomplishes (Luke 9:31) — delivering his people through the waters of death into the promised inheritance. Joseph's bones-instruction is a faith-confession that the story is not over at death and that God's deliverance is bodily and communal.
23: A shadow: by faith Moses' parents hid him for three months, not fearing the king's edict, because they saw he was a fine child. The faith of Moses' parents — seeing his worth against the royal death-decree — is the shadow of the faith that recognizes the worth of Christ when the world seeks to destroy him. Pharaoh's edict to kill Hebrew infant boys corresponds to Herod's massacre of the innocents (Matthew 2:16-18). The preservation of the infant Moses against the killing-king anticipates the preservation of Jesus through Egypt and his emergence as the deliverer.
24: A shadow: by faith Moses, when he had grown up, refused to be known as the son of Pharaoh's daughter. Moses' refusal of Egyptian royal identity in solidarity with the oppressed people is the OT shadow of the Incarnation: the Son of God who did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped but took on the form of a servant (Philippians 2:6-7). The voluntary identity-change — from security and status to solidarity with the suffering — is the shape of both Moses' faith-choice and the Son's redemptive self-emptying. The shadow is real; the antitype is immeasurably greater.
25: A shadow: Moses chose to be mistreated with the people of God rather than enjoy the fleeting pleasures of sin. The choice of suffering-with over comfort-for-self is the shadow of the cross. Christ, for the joy set before him, endured the cross, scorning its shame (Hebrews 12:2). Moses, for the reward (Hebrews 11:26), chose the reproach. The same eschatological orientation — future reward over present pleasure — structures both the shadow (Moses' faith-choice) and the reality (Christ's cross-bearing). The community of Christ is called into the same pattern.
27: A revelation of God: by faith Moses left Egypt, not fearing the king's anger, because he saw him who is invisible. Moses' ability to endure is grounded in seeing the invisible God — the same God who is disclosed definitively in Christ (Hebrews 1:3: the exact imprint of God's nature). The community of Christ similarly sees the invisible (the enthroned Son, the heavenly sanctuary) by faith and is enabled to endure what the visible world threatens. Moses' vision of the invisible God is the OT shadow of the community's vision of Christ by faith and Spirit.
28: A type: by faith Moses kept the Passover and the application of blood, so that the destroyer of the firstborn would not touch them. The Passover blood applied to the doorposts — protecting the Israelite firstborn from the destroying angel — is the structural type of Christ's blood applied to the believer. The destroyer passes over those who are covered; the divine judgment of wrath passes over those who are in Christ, covered by his atoning blood. Paul makes the type explicit (1 Corinthians 5:7); John names the reality (John 1:29). The Passover is the oldest and most structurally precise type of the cross.
29: A shadow: by faith the people passed through the Red Sea as on dry land, but when the Egyptians tried they were drowned. The Red Sea crossing is the shadow of baptism (1 Corinthians 10:2) — the community passing through the waters of death into the new life beyond. The water that delivers God's people destroys their pursuers. Christ's death and resurrection are the reality of which the sea-crossing was the figure: he went through the waters of death and emerged alive, taking his people through with him. Those who are in Christ have already passed through the waters of judgment.
30: A revelation of God: by faith the walls of Jericho fell after the army had marched around them for seven days. The walls-collapse demonstrates God's pattern of victory through non-conventional means — marching and trumpets rather than siege weapons. The enemy's strongholds fall not by human technique but by faith-obedience to God's instruction. The community of Christ trusts the same pattern: the strongholds of sin and death have been demolished by Christ's cross — the apparent foolishness of God that is wiser than human wisdom (1 Corinthians 1:25). The victory is God's; the community marches by faith.
31: A shadow: by faith Rahab the prostitute did not perish with those who were disobedient, because she welcomed the spies in peace. Rahab's faith — expressed in her reception of the Israelite messengers and the application of the scarlet cord in her window — is the shadow of the faith that receives Christ's messengers and is protected by his blood. The scarlet thread marking the house of the saved is a long-noted prefiguration of the blood that marks off those who are covered from those who are not. The Gentile outsider included in the people of God by faith anticipates the new covenant inclusion of all nations in Christ.
32: A revelation of God: what more shall I say? Time would fail me to tell of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, David, Samuel, and the prophets. The compression acknowledges the abundance of faith-witnesses without exhausting them. The figures named — judges, king, prophets — span the entire era of Israel's covenant history, showing that faith-in-God was the constant through every institutional form. In Christ, all these institutional forms find their fulfillment: judge who delivers, king who reigns, prophet who speaks — all offices he holds and surpasses.
33: A revelation of God: through faith they conquered kingdoms, administered justice, gained what was promised, shut the mouths of lions. The catalog of faith-victories reveals the character of the God who responds to faith across the centuries: he delivers from kings, animals, and fire; he gives what he has promised; he establishes justice through his people. The Christ who is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Hebrews 13:8) is the one who worked these victories in the shadow-time and who now works the greater victory over sin and death in the fulfilled time.
34: A revelation of God: weakness was turned to strength, and armies of foreigners were routed. Weakness turned to strength is the resurrection pattern: the one who was weak enough to die on the cross was given the strength of indestructible life (Hebrews 7:16). The OT figures who experienced the weakness-to-strength reversal are the shadows of what Christ achieves definitively and what the community enters eschatologically: "when I am weak, then I am strong" (2 Corinthians 12:10) is the shape of new covenant existence.
35: A direct revelation: women received their dead raised to life again. There were others who were tortured, refusing release, so that they might rise to a better resurrection. The "better resurrection" toward which some OT faithful died without attaining is the resurrection that Christ's own resurrection inaugurates and guarantees. The temporary restorations (1 Kings 17:22; 2 Kings 4:35) were gracious but provisional — the dead raised returned to die again. The better resurrection is the permanent, eschatological raising that is now secure in Christ the firstfruits (1 Corinthians 15:23), from which there is no second dying.
36: A theme: some faced jeers and flogging, chains and imprisonment. The suffering of God's faithful witnesses is the continuous theme of covenant history — the prophets mocked and imprisoned (Jer 37-38; 1 Kings 22:27) are the cloud that surrounds the community. Christ is the prophet above all others who endured jeering, flogging, and imprisonment before his execution. The community that bears the same reproach (Hebrews 13:13) enters the same fellowship with the one who went before them through the same suffering to the same glory.
37: A theme: they were stoned, sawn in two, killed by the sword; they went about in sheepskins and goatskins, destitute, afflicted, mistreated. The extreme suffering and destitution of the faithful witnesses finds its ultimate expression in the cross. The one who had no place to lay his head, who was stripped and executed between criminals, is the final and fullest expression of the faithful-sufferer pattern. The tradition of Isaiah sawn in two, Jeremiah stoned, John the Baptist killed by sword — all point toward the one whose suffering was not merely in the same pattern but was the pattern's reason and goal.
38: A revelation of God: the world was not worthy of them — they wandered in deserts, mountains, and caves. The divine counter-evaluation stands against the world's verdict: those whom the world expelled and deemed unworthy were the ones God valued as the world was not worthy to contain. In Christ, the same divine counter-judgment operates definitively: the one whom the world crucified as worthy only of death, God declared worthy of all honor (Revelation 5:12) by raising him and seating him at the right hand. The resurrection is God's public repudiation of the world's verdict on his Son.
40: A direct revelation: God had planned something better for us so that apart from us they should not be made perfect. The "something better" is the realized eschatology of the new covenant — Christ's atonement, resurrection, and the Spirit — which the OT faithful glimpsed but could not participate in until the community of Christ was also gathered. The perfection is communal and final: Abel, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Rahab, and all the faithful of every age are perfected together at the parousia in Christ. The whole people of faith, from the first to the last, enters the completion as one body — which is why the community's perseverance to the end matters for the whole story of salvation.
